(born 4 January 2000) is a Croatian [pair skater](/wiki/Pair_skater "Pair skater"). With her skating partner, [Antonio Souza-Kordeiru](/wiki/Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru "Antonio Souza-Kordeiru"), she has competed in the final segment at four [European Championships](/wiki/European_Figure_Skating_Championships "European Figure Skating Championships").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Croatian pair skater

Lana Petranović

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Petranović and Souza-Kordeiru in 2018 | |
| Personal information | |
| Born | (2000-01-04) 4 January 2000 (age 26)  [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb"), Croatia |
| Height | 1.47 m (4 ft 10 in) |
| Figure skating career | |
|  | |
| Country | [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") |
| Coach | Dmitri Savin |
| Skating club | KK Medo Zagreb |
| Began skating | 2007 |
|  | |

**Lana Petranović** (born 4 January 2000) is a Croatian [pair skater](/wiki/Pair_skater "Pair skater"). With her skating partner, [Antonio Souza-Kordeiru](/wiki/Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru "Antonio Souza-Kordeiru"), she has competed in the final segment at four [European Championships](/wiki/European_Figure_Skating_Championships "European Figure Skating Championships").

## Skating career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Skating career")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

Petranović began learning to skate in 2007.[[1]](#cite_note-ISU-1819-1) As a singles skater, she appeared at one [ISU Junior Grand Prix](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "ISU Junior Grand Prix") event, in October 2014. She last competed in singles in January 2015, at the [European Youth Olympic Winter Festival](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2015_European_Youth_Olympic_Winter_Festival "Figure skating at the 2015 European Youth Olympic Winter Festival") in [Dornbirn](/wiki/Dornbirn "Dornbirn"), Austria.

Petranović decided to switch to [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating") and formed a partnership with Michael Lueck. The pair trained in the United States.[[2]](#cite_note-RBTH170214-2) They made their international debut in September 2015, at the [Lombardia Trophy](/wiki/Lombardia_Trophy "Lombardia Trophy") in Italy, and competed at six other competitions, including three [ISU Challenger Series](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series") events, before parting ways. Their final competition together was the Hellmut Seibt Memorial in February 2016.[[3]](#cite_note-ISU-LPML-3)

Petranović traveled to [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") to find a new partner.[[2]](#cite_note-RBTH170214-2) She teamed up with Russia's [Antonio Souza-Kordeiru](/wiki/Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru "Antonio Souza-Kordeiru") in spring 2016.[[1]](#cite_note-ISU-1819-1)

### 2016–2017 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2016–2017 season")]

Making their international debut as a team, Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed fourth at the [2016 CS Warsaw Cup](/wiki/2016_CS_Warsaw_Cup "2016 CS Warsaw Cup") in November. They finished fifth at the [2016 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2016_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2016 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb") in December; 15th at the [2017 European Championships](/wiki/2017_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2017 European Figure Skating Championships") in January in [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava"), Czech Republic; and 21st at the [2017 World Championships](/wiki/2017_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2017 World Figure Skating Championships") in March in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland. They were coached by [Yuri Larionov](/wiki/Yuri_Larionov "Yuri Larionov") in Moscow.[[4]](#cite_note-CSF161107-4)[[5]](#cite_note-ISU-1617-5)

### 2017–2018 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2017–2018 season")]

Petranović underwent an operation after injuring the [meniscus](/wiki/Meniscus_(anatomy) "Meniscus (anatomy)") in her knee and returned to the ice after three months.[[6]](#cite_note-RBTH171214-6) Due to her injury, the pair was unable to compete in September at the [2017 CS Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2017_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2017 CS Nebelhorn Trophy"), which served as the final [qualification opportunity for the 2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Qualification "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Qualification"). They returned to competition in January, placing 12th at the [2018 European Championships](/wiki/2018_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2018 European Figure Skating Championships") in Moscow. In March, they placed 21st at the [2018 World Championships](/wiki/2018_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2018 World Figure Skating Championships") in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Italy. They were coached by Larionov and Dmitri Savin in Moscow.[[7]](#cite_note-ISU-1718-7)

### 2018–2019 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2018–2019 season")]

Coached by Savin in Moscow,[[1]](#cite_note-ISU-1819-1) Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed 7th at their first event of the season, the [2018 CS Lombardia Trophy](/wiki/2018_CS_Lombardia_Trophy "2018 CS Lombardia Trophy"). They competed at two other [Challenger events](/wiki/2018-19_ISU_Challenger_Series "2018-19 ISU Challenger Series") of the season, the [2018 CS Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2018_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2018 CS Nebelhorn Trophy") and [2018 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2018_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2018 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb"), placing eighth and seventh. They finished the season placing eighth at the [2019 European Championships](/wiki/2019_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 European Figure Skating Championships") and sixteenth at the [2019 World Championships](/wiki/2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 World Figure Skating Championships").[[8]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-8)

### 2019–2020 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2019–2020 season")]

Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed twelfth at the [2019 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2019_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2019 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb") and fifteenth at the [2020 European Championships](/wiki/2020_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2020 European Figure Skating Championships"). The [2020 World Championships](/wiki/2020_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2020 World Figure Skating Championships") were cancelled as a result of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[8]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-8)

### 2020–2021 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2020–2021 season")]

Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed twenty-first at the [2021 World Championships](/wiki/2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 World Figure Skating Championships").[[8]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-8)

### 2021–2022 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lana_Petranovi%C4%87&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2021–2022 season")]

Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru began the season at the [2021 CS Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2021_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2021 CS Nebelhorn Trophy"), seeking to [qualify](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Qualification "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Qualification") a berth at the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics"). They placed twelfth, outside of qualification. They went on to finish sixth at the [Budapest Trophy](/wiki/Budapest_Trophy "Budapest Trophy") and eleventh at the [2021 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2021_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2021 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb"), and then sixteenth at the [2022 European Championships](/wiki/2022_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2022 European Figure Skating Championships").[[8]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-8)

In February, Souza-Kordeiru announced his retirement from competitive figure skating, ending their partnership.[[9]](#cite_note-sk-ret-9)
